# Common Failure Patterns

Use these labels so the audit names the real problem.

## 1. Same-value-shift plateau

Several adjacent units all move in the same emotional or tactical direction.

Example shape:
- "we are later than we thought"
- "institutions fail again"
- "the threat is even bigger"

One or two can work. A long run at the same pitch flattens.

## 2. Duplicate chapter function

Two chapters do basically the same job:
- both are return/travel chapters
- both are fallout chapters
- both are "build the plan" chapters
- both are courtroom failure chapters

Different scenery does not rescue duplicated function.

## 3. Sequel overload

Too many chapters or scenes are processing, debating, mourning, recalculating, or explaining without enough fresh external turn.

This is especially costly late in the story.

## 4. False urgency

Characters talk like the clock matters, but the structure does not change:
- no new cost
- no lost option
- no worsened position
- no faster consequence

Clock language without structural change reads like noise.

## 5. Dead travel

Movement happens, but the journey does not add:
- new information
- bodily cost
- world consequence
- relational change
- tactical narrowing

If the only function is "get there," the unit may be too long or in the wrong place.

## 6. Underbuilt climax

Too much setup energy reaches the peak, but the peak itself is brief, indirect, or overexplained.

Symptoms:
- climax arrives after too much discussion
- best turns happen in summary rather than in-scene
- the reader waits longer than the payoff earns

## 7. Overbuilt aftermath / coda

The climax already answered the main dramatic question, but multiple chapters continue restating the same emotional or political result.

Ask whether each aftermath unit adds a new vector or only longer residue.

## 8. Breathlessness without contrast

Every unit is pitched as urgent, loud, or catastrophic. Without contrast, the story feels monotonous anyway.

## 9. Misdiagnosed quiet

The user calls something slow, but the chapter is actually doing necessary stillness work:
- changing the relationship
- naming the cost
- giving the next plan shape
- creating contrast before the peak

Do not "fix" the chapter until you check whether it is the only stillness beat doing real work.

## 10. Concept-density plateau

Important material keeps arriving, but several adjacent units are all doing explanation, philosophical pressure, institutional discovery, or investigative deepening at roughly the same pitch.

This often shows up in dense middle sections:
- split threads that are both idea-heavy
- chapters that all feel like "go deeper, learn more, get worse"
- long conversations or discoveries that matter but blur together

The result is a section that looks busy on paper but does not feel like it is accelerating.

Test:
- write a one-line job for each chapter
- if the jobs are only different in scenery or amount of information, the run may be blurred

## 11. Double fallout blur

Two adjacent units both process the consequence of the same event.

Common shape:
- first chapter opens the wound
- second chapter explains the wound again

This is not automatically wrong. It works when the second unit adds a new vector:
- movement
- decision
- outside pressure
- redirected objective

If the second unit only lengthens the residue, the aftermath is probably overbuilt.
